extra.message: User input from request.POST.get('first_name') is base64-decoded and passed directly to exec(). This allows arbitrary remote code execution on the server. An attacker can encode any Python code in base64 and submit it via the form to gain full control of the server.

cwe: CWE-94: Improper Control of Generation of Code ('Code Injection')

extra.message: Python 2 fallback path: user input from request.POST.get('first_name') is base64-decoded and passed directly to exec(). This is a duplicate code path for the same arbitrary code execution vulnerability.

cwe: CWE-94: Improper Control of Generation of Code ('Code Injection')

extra.message: User input from request.GET.get('p') is joined to a base path via os.path.join() and passed to open() without any sanitization or path validation. An attacker can use '../' sequences to traverse the filesystem and read arbitrary files (e.g., ?p=../../etc/passwd).

cwe: CWE-22: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal')

extra.message: User input from request.GET.get('url') is passed directly to django.shortcuts.redirect() without any validation. An attacker can craft a URL parameter pointing to a malicious external site, causing the application to redirect authenticated users to phishing pages.

cwe: CWE-601: URL Redirection to Untrusted Site ('Open Redirect')

extra.message: User input from request.GET.get('fwd') is used to look up and call arbitrary functions via globals().get(). An attacker can invoke any function defined in the views module (or imported into its global scope) by name, including os.unlink or other dangerous functions. This is effectively a code execution vulnerability through arbitrary function dispatch.

cwe: CWE-94: Improper Control of Generation of Code ('Code Injection')

extra.message: The dor_user_profile view allows any unauthenticated user to access and modify any user's profile by changing the userid parameter in the URL (e.g., /direct-object-references/users/2). No authentication or authorization check is performed before reading or writing user data.

cwe: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

extra.message: The missing_access_control view grants admin access based solely on a user-controlled query parameter (?action=admin) without any authentication or authorization check. Any user can access the admin page by simply appending the parameter to the URL.

User input from the query string parameter 'qs' is rendered inside a <script> block with the |safe filter ({{qs|safe}}), completely disabling Django's auto-escaping. An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript by crafting a URL like ?qs=";alert(document.cookie);//.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: User-controlled URL path variable 'path' is rendered inside a <script> block with the |safe filter ({{path|safe}}), disabling auto-escaping. An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript through a crafted URL path.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: User input from the query string parameter 'qs' is rendered in an HTML input value attribute with the |safe filter (value="{{qs|safe}}"), disabling auto-escaping. An attacker can break out of the attribute and inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript (e.g., ?qs="><script>alert(1)</script>).

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: User input from the query string parameter 'qs' is rendered inside a <script> block with the |safe filter ({{qs|safe}}), disabling auto-escaping. Combined with the @csrf_exempt decorator on this view, this enables both XSS and CSRF attacks.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: The Django CSRF middleware (django.middleware.csrf.CsrfViewMiddleware) is commented out in MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES. This globally disables CSRF protection for all views, allowing cross-site request forgery attacks against all POST endpoints in the application.

The csrf_image view is explicitly decorated with @csrf_exempt, disabling CSRF protection. Combined with the globally disabled CSRF middleware, this view is completely unprotected from cross-site request forgery.

The Django SECRET_KEY is hardcoded in the settings file and committed to version control. This key is used for cryptographic signing of sessions, CSRF tokens, and other security-sensitive operations. An attacker with knowledge of this key can forge sessions, tamper with signed data, and potentially achieve remote code execution through deserialization attacks.

DEBUG = True is set in Django settings. In production, this exposes detailed error pages with stack traces, local variables, settings, and database queries to any user who triggers an error. The boom() view at /misconfiguration/boom intentionally raises an exception to demonstrate this information leak.

cwe: CWE-209: Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information

extra.message: The Django Debug Toolbar is enabled and its visibility is controlled by a user-supplied 'debug' parameter (via GET, POST, or cookie). Any external user can activate the debug toolbar by adding ?debug=1 to any request, exposing SQL queries, settings, request data, templates, and other sensitive internal information.

cwe: CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor

extra.message: The XFrameOptionsMiddleware is commented out in MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES. This allows the application to be embedded in iframes on any domain, enabling clickjacking attacks where an attacker overlays invisible frames to trick users into performing unintended actions.

cwe: CWE-1021: Improper Restriction of Rendered UI Layers or Frames

extra.message: A cookie named 'monster' is set via response.set_cookie() without the HttpOnly, Secure, or SameSite flags. Without HttpOnly, the cookie is accessible to JavaScript (enabling theft via XSS). Without Secure, the cookie may be transmitted over unencrypted HTTP. Without SameSite, the cookie is vulnerable to CSRF.

The boom() view intentionally raises an unhandled exception. Combined with DEBUG=True in settings, this exposes a full Django debug error page containing stack traces, local variables, Django settings (including SECRET_KEY), installed apps, and middleware configuration to any unauthenticated user.

cwe: CWE-209: Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information
